text: Robotic assisted coiled sleeve gastrectomy to ruin wide gastric bypass. The patient is a forty-six year old female with history of severe gastroesophageal reflux and severe dysphagia after a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy performed in two thousand eighteen. The workup included an upper GI swallow that showed a kinked sleeve in two areas and an EGD that confirmed the findings.

The surgeon begins the operation by dissecting the sleeve gastrectomy, separating it from the omentum and dividing the additions at the suture line. The hiatus is carefully dissected, and a Penrose drain is passed around the retrosophageal window. During the detailed dissection of the hiatus, a previous hernia repair with VLOG suture is identified.

The surgeon dissects the esophagus into the mediastinum, achieving three centimeters of intra-abdominal esophagus. The hiatus is closed using two O-etibon sutures. An intraoperative EGD is performed to check for kinks or structures at the proximal sleeve. The sleeve is transected approximately five centimeters from the gastroesophageal junction, and the antrum is resected.
The surgeon performs a jejuno-jejunostomy, followed by a two-layer gastrojejunostomy, creating an 80-centimeter alimentary limb and a 50-centimeter biliopancreatic limb.

After the Roux-en-Y gastric bypass was finished, a new intraoperative EGD was made to assure the indemnity of the gastrointestinal stone. The operative time was two hundred and fifty minutes and the blood loss was minimal. The patient was discharged on day number one after an upper GI swallow that showed good passage without evidence of a leak and tolerating a blenderized diet.
